---
title: "RS ALL WEEKS"
format: html
editor: visual
---

Background

I have experience working with commercial businesses and in local and regional government, identifying sites for inward investment and applying GIS techniques to optimise operations and realise efficiencies. The skills I will acquire throughout the course will directly apply to the work I carry out and will improve the efficiency of data handling and access. This will increase the accessibility of management information across the organisation. This module will enhance existing methods of information sharing and demonstrate how new data can be created from existing imagery that is available but not currently analysed spatially, either because it is in the wrong format or inaccessible to those who could make best use of it.

-   1 - intro to remote sensing

-   Week 2 - See Presentation link(s) and 1 page image.

-   

<https://github.com/nolanj25/RS_Week2/blob/master/FINAL_FILE_RUN.Rmd>

[FINAL_FILE_RUN.html · nolanj25/RS_Week2\@2a890](https://github.com/nolanj25/RS_Week2/commit/2a890ce28057c50b6202caf0ebce4a1f82be5813)

![](images/clipboard-101575709.png)

-   *3 - corrections*

-   *4 - Policy (individual case study city)*

-   *5 - Google Earth Engine*

-   *6 - classification 1*

-   *7 - classification 2*

-   *8 - SAR or temperature, or combination*

<!-- -->

-   1 - intro to remote sensing

Remote sensing in Week 1 introduced the distinction between passive and active data collection, which forms the foundation for understanding satellite imagery. Passive systems rely on sunlight as their illumination source, while active systems—such as LiDAR, radar, or X‑ray sensors—emit their own energy and measure the returned signal. This week also highlighted how atmospheric conditions influence the quality of remotely sensed data. Phenomena such as Rayleigh scattering explain why shorter wavelengths scatter more easily, producing effects like the blue appearance of oceans or the reddening of sunlight. Cloud cover, smoke, and volcanic ash act as filters that can obscure or distort imagery, raising questions about whether high‑cloud‑cover scenes can be selectively filtered out during data searches.

A major focus was on the different data types and formats used in remote sensing. Raster data, such as GeoTIFFs, contrast with LiDAR point‑cloud data, which record X, Y, Z and intensity values to generate DEMs and DTMs. We also explored spectral concepts including the red‑edge region, multispectral versus hyperspectral imaging, and the construction of true‑colour composites. Understanding sensor resolution is essential: MODIS provides 500 m resolution, Landsat offers 30 m, and Sentinel‑2 improves this to 10 m. Using SNAP software demonstrated how differences in weather, spatial resolution, and surface reflectance (SR) influence the interpretation of Landsat and Sentinel imagery.

This week also encouraged independent thought about practical applications and data‑handling challenges. Image size and processing requirements can limit what analyses are feasible, making repeatable workflows important. Comparing images of different resolutions or dates requires consistent settings, especially when studying vegetation recovery or environmental change. The infrared reflectance of plants—linked to evolutionary adaptations—was identified as a promising area for exploring regrowth after volcanic events. Image manipulation, transparency adjustments, and the difficulty of locating specific scenes across satellites with varying colour coverage and date ranges were also noted as practical obstacles.

This week raised questions about automation and reproducibility in remote sensing workflows. It would be useful to specify latitude, longitude, and zoom scale directly to retrieve consistent imagery across time, perhaps using R to store these parameters. This could support long‑term monitoring, such as analysing monthly vegetation change from 1994–2026 or comparing plume heights and crater features using satellite and oblique photographs. Historical mapping from the UCL Geography Department could be georeferenced and compared with modern satellite data to study landscape change. Additional topics of interest include the availability of InSAR for measuring height change and the possibility that cloud shadows may reveal otherwise hidden volcanic activity, as seen in events like the 3 November 2008 eruption.

After downloading data from various sources, I’ve noticed several issues. The file sizes are very large, and multiple images often need to be blended together to create a true, seamless image. The resolution is also lower than I expected based on my initial Sentinel‑2 download. Processing time has become a problem as well. Ideally, I’d like to be able to select smaller, more manageable image areas.

![](images/clipboard-3193432266.png)

**Week 2 - See Presentation link URL (check)**

Portfolio, a short xaringan (or Quarto) presentation on sensor

RS_Week2/FINAL_FILE_RUN_files at master · nolanj25/RS_Week2

Image in QGIS - with full 3 band colour and with Geo-referenced background map

![](images/clipboard-2894018029.png)

![](images/clipboard-1080931233.png)

***Week 3*** - corrections - notes on *Georeferencing and Regression*

-   *Georeferencing involves aligning a scanned image with an existing image or a coordinate reference system.*

-   *To assign a coordinate reference system, Ground Control Points (GCPs) are collected.*

-   *A gold‑standard map is compared with the scanned image, and a regression plot is created. Many points are needed — the more, the better. See ther Rabaul volcano example sourcing image and quality mapping*

-   *Several regression methods (around 5–6) can be used for georeferencing. QGIS was used to add the layers*

-   *PIFs (Pseudo‑Invariant Features) help maintain consistency across images. like roads rivers ect - roads were used used in the Rabaul volcanoe example as well at the old Air port runway.*

-   *Resampling methods include nearest neighbour and centroid‑based approaches. ( this has not been applied yet)*

-   *Non‑matching grids must be matched before analysis, and images need to be the same size. (The over lapping areas match with different size images)*

-   *Top‑of‑Atmosphere* TOA spectral radiance is light reflected off the Earth as seen from the satellite measure in radiance units.

***Atmospheric Data and Corrections***

-   *Pixel “bleeding” occurs when light from the Earth’s surface spreads into neighbouring pixels, especially across images taken over time.*

-   *Dark Object Subtraction is one method of atmospheric correction, though not ideal.*

-   *PIF‑based correction uses a reference image. (See above map)*

-   *Corrections are applied per band: the reference pixel value is applied to all corresponding pixels in the target image.*

-   *Regression is applied on a per‑band basis.*

-   *Care is needed when interpreting charts, as y‑values may differ.*

***Absolute Atmospheric Correction***

-   *Models the atmosphere directly and applies the model to the image.*

-   *Introduces uncertainty and bias; requires weather data.*

-   *Different biomes require different atmospheric models.*

-   *Tools include free and paid software such as FLASH and ENVI.*

-   *Empirical Line Correction uses in‑situ reflectance measurements.*

-   *Atmospheric path radiance and attenuation are both accounted for in the correction equation.*

-   *Analysis‑ready data is often used to avoid these complexities.*

***Topographic Correction***

-   *Solar zenith describes the sun’s height above the horizon.*

-   *Nadir view is directly downward; off‑nadir angles (e.g., 25°) affect illumination*

-   *The MTL file contains metadata such as tile angle, sun angle, and acquisition date.*

-   *Solar azimuth describes the sun’s horizontal position (0° = north, 90° = east).*

-   *Topographic correction accounts for terrain elevation and slope.*

-   *Atmospheric correction is applied first, then topographic correction*

***Radiometric Correction***

-   *Digital Numbers (DNs) are efficient for storage and processing.*

-   *DN represents the amount of light detected by the sensor.*

-   *Conversion uses bias and gain values (e.g., from the Landsat handbook).*

-   *Spectral radiance → reflectance.*

-   *Radiance is influenced by the light source; reflectance accounts for the light source and represents the surface properties.*

-   *Top‑of‑Atmosphere (TOA) reflectance is commonly used.*

-   *Apparent reflectance differs from pure hemispherical reflectance*

***Data Products and Processing***

-   *Landsat products are provided at different collection levels.*

-   *“Load surface collection” refers to using surface reflectance products.*

-   *Image mosaicking involves joining tiles using feathering or blending.*

-   *Collections (e.g., in Google Earth Engine) allow stacking images and taking median values over a time period (e.g., 3 months).*

***Enhancement and Analysis***

-   *Enhancements include contrast stretching and other pixel‑based adjustments*

-   *Ratios and filters (e.g., NDVI, red‑edge indices) extract spectral information.*

-   *PCA (Principal Component Analysis) reduces dimensionality (e.g., 7 bands → 2 components) while retaining variance*

-   *PCA helps identify change, such as new buildings.*

-   *Texture analysis uses second‑order statistics (co‑occurrence matrices).*

-   *Multiple texture measures exist (around 15), including mean, variance, and directional change.*

-   *Texture analysis is computationally heavy, especially over large areas*

***Correction and Interpretation***

-   *Images contain errors; corrections help ensure values (e.g., reflectance or DN) are meaningful*

-   *When joining datasets, features may need to be extracted using ratios or other transformations.*

**4 - Policy (individual case study city)**

Heat Prevention Case Study (India)

I initially planned to download imagery for London because I’m familiar with the area, ( see image) but I decided instead to explore a different region in IndiaA case study on heat‑prevention planning in India, which focused on informal settlements (slums).The study examined a 2010 heatwave that showed increased mortality on the hottest days. However, the report also noted that other factors—such as poor air quality, dengue fever, and general illness—may have contributed to the rise in deaths, not only heat stroke or heat exhaustion. The study did not mention satellite data; it relied mainly on weather‑event monitoring and air‑quality information

This raised an interesting question: could previous satellite imagery, weather data, and air‑quality sensor data be used to support early‑warning systems for a city of seven million people

The city now provides five‑day heat forecasts, and the media reports these warnings.

The Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation pioneered an early‑warning system for extreme heat.

**Additional Considerations**

Air‑pollution monitoring should be integrated with heat‑risk analysis. See later weeks SAR reference.

Spatial mapping of deaths, combined with town‑planning data and the distribution of slums, could reveal patterns of vulnerability.

This could help identify neighbourhoods most at risk and improve targeted interventions.

One quarter of the slums are migrant population and threat with exposure to the heat eg police and rikshaw drivers are susceptible .

The temp range is not large for only 5 degrees ie 40 the 45 degree c. this would apper to be too small. An earlier warning and a wider temperature range, especially with those in countries not used to heat.

Artis & Carnahan (1982), recently summarised by Guha et al. 2018 and and Avdan and Jovanovska (2016)

Heat affects health through dehydration, acute by heat exhaustion and heat stroke, and exacerbations of chronic cardiovascular and respiratory diseases. poor air quality, driven by industrial expansion can worsen during extreme heat and lead to respiratory and other illnesses. Heat is also known to fuel outbreaks of vector-borne diseases such as malaria, chikungunya and dengue fever, all prevalent in Ahmedabad \[[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4024996/#B6-ijerph-11-03473)\], and to impact the incidence of diarrheal disease, especially among children.

-   ***5 - Google Earth Engine***

*Add GEE Pratical codes and Images*

Loaded code to displayBoundaries and

Loaded Images

Centering mapinng with coodinates and pre defined features

GGE

London Borough of Tower Hamlets, United Kingdom Feb 14, 2026 Satellite Imagery

Example of own area data Upload (GEE code)

**A good reference used with code**

Running the book’s example code in GEE works well and allows other case studies to be explored see image below. This will take time but will be valuable for deeper learning.

(Cardille,Crowley, Saah, Clinton (Editors)

![](images/clipboard-209908950.png)

**Week 6**

An increasing variety of remotely sensed data is becoming available, from very high spatial resolution images such as QuickBird and WorldView to very coarse spatialresolution data obtained from the MODerate resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer 346luetal.(MODIS) and the Advanced Very High Resolution Radiometer (AVHRR). Spectralresolution ranges from the limited number of multispectral bands of the LandsatThematic Mapper (TM) to the over 200 bands of the hyperspectral Hyperion. Passiveoptical sensors such as Landsat and SPOT can be contrasted with active sensorssuch as the Advanced Land Observation Satellite (ALOS) Phased Array type L-bandSynthetic Aperture Radar (PALSAR) and RADARSAT.

-   ***7 - classification 2***

-   Example of running GEE code with land sat imagery

![](images/clipboard-654671235.png)

**Week 8** SAR or temperature, or combination

Loaded and displayed in GEE and researched the SAR properties for Identifying different in structural bodies using active sensor reluctance for heat identification in urban areas. Linking the practical application with past studies and theory

Whilst multispectral sensors passively measure electromagnetic radiation reflected from the Earth's surface, radar sensors are active, meaning they emit electromagnetic radiation and then measure the returning signal. Multispectral sensors capture information on chemical properties of surfaces, such as nitrogen or carbon content and moisture (Asner, [**1998**](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0002 "https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0002"); Tempfli, Kerle, Huurneman, & Janssen, [**2009**](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0104 "https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0104")), 

**Practical – Week 8** This week focused on splitting the Level‑3 boundaries and adding a mask to the data before loading it, as well as converting the temperature data from Kelvin to Celsius. This was of use in understanding the group work to explaine conversion of raw data to a simplified measure.

The code loaded and ran successfully, producing an impressive output in both coverage and granularity. The cooler appearance of the rivers immediately increased confidence in the imagery. Colour parameters were taken from MODIS.

The resulting image is somewhat blurry and less spatially precise, but it provides strong areal representation of warmer and cooler regions. This is because the MODIS collection contains more images to draw temperature data from. The output is visually appealing and useful for comparing different regions, as well as for higher‑level policy perspectives.

This week highlighted the importance of interpreting images and understanding how they are captured, processed, and produced. The best sensor to use depends entirely on the specific question being asked. For flooding as an example it is not easy to identify. Any surface that is large and smooth can appear as water in SAR imagery due to specular reflectance, leading to misclassification. Examples include airports, exposed channel beds, and highways. Seasonal flooding—regular, *expected annual flooding*—must also be considered.

And there are muti sensor satalites that can capture the vertical column measurements of NO2, for example. The troposphere is the closest we can get to ground-level measurements with Sentinel-5P. (Zander, Venter,Sourangsu, Chowdhury)

There is a clear need for training in image interpretation, as well as in loading, manipulating, and understanding imagery. Further reading and analysis will help answer both existing and emerging questions.

An area of interest is whether ancient buildings in Egypt—and associated mining works or structures—could be identified. This would require understanding what features to look for before searching the imagery, unless code were developed to detect rubble signatures, columns, or other characteristic pixel patterns.

![](images/clipboard-315653524.png)

**References**

*Cloud-Based Remote Sensing with Google Earth Engine: Fundamentals and Applications* Jeffrey A. Cardille, Morgan A. Crowley, David Saah, Nicholas E. Clinton (Editors)

***Other Image providers exist See link***

Lu, D., Li, G., Moran, E., Dutra, L., & Batistella, M. (2011). A Comparison of Multisensor Integration Methods for Land Cover Classification in the Brazilian Amazon. *GIScience & Remote Sensing*, *48*(3), 345–370. <https://doi.org/10.2747/1548-1603.48.3.345>

Zander Venter and Sourangsu Chowdhury <https://eos.com/landviewer/?lat=51.83581&lng=0.47917&z=8&datasets=5&id=S2C_tile_20260214_30UXC_0&b=Red,Green,Blue&anti=true&processing=L2A>

**Dongying Li, Galen D Newman, Bev Wilson, Yue Zhang, Robert D Brown**

[[**Modeling the relationships between historical redlining, urban heat, and heat-related emergency department visits: An examination of 11 Texas cities**]{.underline}](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/23998083211039854 "https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/23998083211039854")

(Asner, [**1998**](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0002 "https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0002"); Tempfli, Kerle, in

Schulte to Bühne H, Pettorelli N. Better together: Integrating and fusing multispectral and radar satellite imagery to inform biodiversity monitoring, ecological research and conservation science. Methods Ecol Evol. 2018;9:849–865. <https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.12942>

Huurneman, & Janssen, [**2009**](https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0104 "https://besjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/2041-210X.12942#mee312942-bib-0104")), 

Schulte to Bühne H, Pettorelli N. Better together: Integrating and fusing multispectral and radar satellite imagery to inform biodiversity monitoring, ecological research and conservation science. Methods Ecol Evol. 2018;9:849–865. <https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.12942>

Zander Venter and Sourangsu Chowdhury

<https://www.eefabook.org/go-to-the-book.html>

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
1 + 2
```

You can add options to executable code like this

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).
